text: For medication-order verification tasks, search broadly enough to capture all potentially relevant active orders before deciding none exist; do not rely on a single narrow category if duplicates could appear in other medication categories.
When the task requires keeping exactly one order, inspect returned medication details and timestamps to identify matching or duplicate therapies and determine the newest order before taking action.
Do not claim a chart state was fully verified unless the available queries actually checked all relevant resources and constraints needed for that conclusion.
Before planning to discontinue, modify, or clean up duplicates, confirm the available API supports that action; if it does not, avoid promising unsupported changes and state the limitation clearly.
Use the most direct resource and task-specific search parameters first; avoid broad paginated searches unless targeted queries fail and are truly necessary.
Before placing an order that depends on prior clinical evidence, verify the triggering condition from the data and state the exact evidence used.
When a primary search returns no results, use the instructed fallback criteria exactly (such as matching text/code hints) instead of exploring unrelated filters or categories.
Stop searching once you have enough confirmed information to answer the question or act safely; do not continue low-yield queries that do not improve the decision.
Do not state that a condition has been verified after an intervention unless you re-check the record or the API response itself proves the final state.
When a task defines duplicates broadly, search and reason over the full relevant class of orders, not just the exact named item or a narrow subtype.
Before planning corrective actions, confirm the available API supports the needed operation; if discontinue/update is unavailable, avoid claiming completion of that step.
In the final response, describe only what was actually checked and changed, using wording that matches the task’s exact success criterion.
After creating, updating, or discontinuing records to satisfy a target count, explicitly confirm the final state meets the requested condition (for example, exactly one active order remains).
Base the final answer on both the retrieved data and any write action performed, summarizing the verification result rather than only describing the action taken.
When a task specifies a precise end condition, mirror that wording in the completion message to show the requirement was fully checked and satisfied.
Emit exactly one valid API action per turn, matching the required response format with no extra text or concatenated actions.
Resolve the patient first, then use the confirmed patient identifier/reference format expected by downstream searches and writes.
Do not declare a task impossible before exhausting the available retrieval steps and confirming what the API can or cannot do from the provided schema.
When fixing or verifying orders, base the decision on actual returned data from the relevant resource search before creating, modifying, or concluding completion.
After making an API call, wait for the returned data before deciding, summarizing, or taking the next action.
